coredata.dc:title: Bright white light emission from blue emitting carbon dot-coated Dy3+-doped luminescent glasses
coredata.dc:creator: Özlem, Beyza
Korkmaz, Ulaş
Erol, Erdinç
Alaş, Melis Özge
Meray, Zeynep
Genç Altürk, Rükan
Çelikbilek Ersundu, Miray
Ersundu, Ali Erçin
coredata.dc:description: Dy3+-doped luminescent glasses have received great attention due to their ability to emit white light at a suitable yellow-to-blue intensity ratio. However, achieving bright white light using single Dy3+-doped glasses remains a challenge due to the hypersensitivity of the emission band at 575 nm — usually resulting in intense yellow emission. In this work, we present a novel approach for compensating for the blue emission deficiency of Dy3+ to produce resin-free white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) by synthesizing two series of Dy3+-doped glasses from tellurite and silicate systems on which blue-emitting carbon dots (BCDs) are spin-coated. The structural, chemical, optical, and luminescence properties of tellurite and silicate glasses are compared and discussed in detail. White light emissions are obtained upon 365-nm excitation for BCD-coated Dy3+-doped tellurite and silicate glasses with color coordinates of (x = 0.31, y = 0.33) and (x = 0.31, y = 0.34) and correlated color temperatures (CCT) of 5518 K and 5316 K, respectively. BCDs coating increases photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) values from 3.10 % to 5.62 % and from 20.81 % to 31.49 % for tellurite and silicate glasses, respectively. Ultimately, the findings in this work show the potential of BCD-coated luminescent glasses with excellent luminescent properties to be considered in solid-state lighting applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luminescent glass
Dy3+
Tellurite
Silicate
Carbon dots
White light

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of BCDs and BCDs/PVP solution
Spin-coating of BCDs/PVP solution on Dy3+-doped luminescent glasses
PL emission measurements and colorimetric properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of a) Luminescent glass synthesis, b) BCDs/PVP solution synthesis, and c) Spin-coating of BCDs/PVP solution on luminescent glasses.
Deconvoluted a) C1s, b) N1s, and c) O1s XPS spectra of BCDs.
a) General bright-field TEM image, and b) Particle size distribution histogram of BCDs.
Cross-sectional SEM images of a) TD3–3 L and b) SD05–3 L samples, respectively.
Absorbance spectra of a) TD3 and TD3–3 L, b) SD05 and SD05–3 L samples (inset shows digital images of uncoated and coated luminescent glasses under daylight).
PL excitation spectra of a) BCDs/PVP solution, b) TD3, and c) SD05 glasses.
a) PL spectra, and b) the corresponding CIE color chromaticity diagram of BCDs/PVP solution (Inset shows a digital image of the BCDs/PVP solution under 365 nm excitation).
PL spectra, macro images, and CIE color chromaticity diagrams of a), b), c) TD3, and TD3–3 L, d), e), f) SD05 and SD05–3 L glasses under 365 nm excitation, respectively.

Temperature-dependent PL spectrum, heating-cooling cycles and time-dependent PLQY spectrum of a), b), c) TD3–3 L, and d), e), f) SD05–3 L glasses under 365 nm excitation, respectively (Inset shows macro images of glasses taken under daylight on different days).

Comparison of the CIE color coordinates, CCT, CRI, color purity, and PLQY values of the prepared glasses with some recently reported studies on Dy3+-doped glasses for white light generation along with commercial LEDs.

head.ce:title: Bright white light emission from blue emitting carbon dot-coated Dy3+-doped luminescent glasses

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Engineering, Department of Chemical Engineering, Mersin University, 33110 Mersin, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Engineering, Department of Chemical Engineering, Mersin University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sabanci University, Nanotechnology Research and Application Centre (SUNUM), TR-34956, Istanbul, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sabanci University, Nanotechnology Research and Application Centre (SUNUM)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Sabanci University, Nanotechnology Research and Application Centre (SUNUM), TR-34956, Istanbul, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dy3+-doped luminescent glasses have received great attention due to their ability to emit white light at a suitable yellow-to-blue intensity ratio. However, achieving bright white light using single Dy3+-doped glasses remains a challenge due to the hypersensitivity of the emission band at 575 nm — usually resulting in intense yellow emission. In this work, we present a novel approach for compensating for the blue emission deficiency of Dy3+ to produce resin-free white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) by synthesizing two series of Dy3+-doped glasses from tellurite and silicate systems on which blue-emitting carbon dots (BCDs) are spin-coated. The structural, chemical, optical, and luminescence properties of tellurite and silicate glasses are compared and discussed in detail. White light emissions are obtained upon 365-nm excitation for BCD-coated Dy3+-doped tellurite and silicate glasses with color coordinates of (x = 0.31, y = 0.33) and (x = 0.31, y = 0.34) and correlated color temperatures (CCT) of 5518 K and 5316 K, respectively. BCDs coating increases photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) values from 3.10 % to 5.62 % and from 20.81 % to 31.49 % for tellurite and silicate glasses, respectively. Ultimately, the findings in this work show the potential of BCD-coated luminescent glasses with excellent luminescent properties to be considered in solid-state lighting applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lightning accounts for a significant portion of worldwide electricity consumption. Thus, the development of environmentally friendly and energy-efficient lighting sources is one of the most effective ways to reduce energy consumption and CO2 emissions [1,2]. Herein, white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) for solid-state lighting technology in particular have the potential to decrease energy use due to their high luminous efficiency, longer operating life, environmentally friendly components, and low power consumption [3,4]. The majority of commercial WLEDs are constructed with yellow phosphors coated with epoxy resin on blue LED chips [5]. However, these systems usually suffer from some major disadvantages, mostly originating from phosphorous and resin such as, self-quenching, reabsorption, and high refractive index difference that causes significant light scattering losses [6]. As a consequence of this, phosphor in glass (PiG), which is basically a mixture of phosphor and glass in powder form, comes forward. After sintering, PiG can be placed on top of LED chips as color converters for WLED applications. However, the thermal and chemical stability of PiGs are usually insufficient to meet the recent LED requirements [7–9]. Alternatively, trivalent rare-earth (RE3+) ions-doped luminescent glasses have emerged as promising color converters for WLEDs owing to their high optical transmittance, simple manufacturing techniques, high lanthanide solubility, and low cost [10–13]. Among various RE3+ ions, Dy3+ with 4 f9 electron configuration consists mainly of two bands in the blue (~484 nm) and yellow (~575 nm) regions of the visible spectrum and can therefore directly emit white light at an appropriate yellow-to-blue intensity ratio [14–16]. Hence, many studies have been reported on Dy3+ doping for white light generation in different glass systems such as, silicate [17], tellurite [18], borogermanate [19], phosphate [20] and fluorophosphate [21]. However, the emission band at 575 nm is strongly affected by the local environment due to its hypersensitivity resulting in an intense yellow emission in several glass systems [22,23]. Although doping of Dy3+ together with blue-emitting RE3+ ions such as Tm3+ is considered a promising method to increase the blue emission intensity, achieving brilliant white light emission remains a challenge due to the low down-conversion emission efficiency of Tm3+ [24,25].

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have taken attention as a promising alternative to inorganic luminescence materials due to their fascinating properties including strong and wide optical absorption, tunable emission in the visible range, low toxicity, high emission efficiency, and cheap up-scale production [26,27]. In light of these outstanding characteristics, CDs have been widely utilized in light-emitting devices [28–30]. However, developing multiple-color-emissive solid-state CDs remains a difficulty due to the substantial self-quenching of the CDs in the aggregation or solid-state, which is a result of the aggregation-induced quenching effect (AIQ). [31–33]. At this point, alternative approaches must be introduced for the effective use of CDs in solid-state applications. Recently, Başak and Kayahan produced white light emitting nanostructured composites after annealing of aged thermally oxidized porous silicon coated by green synthesized carbon nanostructures [34]. In another study, He et al. synthesized flaky-like Tm3+-doped zinc borate composites with improved photoluminescent performance by incorporating yellow N-doped carbon dots for NUV-WLEDs [35]. However, to date, to the best of our knowledge, no such study has been focused on the combination of luminescent glasses with carbon dots for their potential use in WLEDs. Therefore, in this work combination of Dy3+-doped glasses with blue-emitting carbon dots (BCDs) is proposed as an effective new strategy to compensate for the low blue light deficiency of Dy3+ ions. Hence, two series of Dy3+-doped glasses are synthesized from different glass systems, i.e., tellurite and silicate, and spin-coated with BCDs. Accordingly, chemical, structural, optical, and photoluminescence (PL) properties of BCD-coated Dy3+-doped tellurite and silicate glasses are investigated in detail. Consequently, both of the BCD-coated Dy3+-doped luminescent glasses show promising luminescence performance and therefore are evaluated as alternative candidates for bright white light generation.

Two different sets of Dy3+-doped samples are synthesized using conventional melt-quenching technique (see Fig. 1a) in tellurite and silicate glass systems with the molar compositions of 25Li2O–25WO3–(50-x)TeO2–xDy2O3 (x = 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4 mol %) and 15Na2O–20CaO–15B2O3–5Al2O3–(45-x)SiO2–xDy2O3 (x = 0.25, 0.5, 1 mol %), respectively. High purity chemicals (99.99 %) purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and Alfa Aesar are weighed using a high precision scale (10−4 g) and thoroughly mixed in an agate mortar with a pestle to ensure complete homogeneity. Thereafter, tellurite and silicate batches are placed into platinum crucibles and melted at 800 °C and 1150 °C for 30 min and 75 min, respectively under an air atmosphere. The obtained melts are poured into pre-heated stainless-steel molds and pressed with another steel plate to obtain as-cast samples. Annealing conditions of as-cast samples are chosen differently due to different glass transition behavior of TeO2 and SiO2-based compositions (see Fig. S1). Accordingly, annealing is realized in a pre-heated furnace below the glass transition temperature of each glass system at 310 °C for 3 h and 450 °C for 5 h for as-cast tellurite and silicate samples, respectively to eliminate residual internal stress originating from the rapid cooling of melts. After confirming the amorphous nature of as-cast samples by XRD analysis (see Fig. S2) glass samples are ground and polished with an average thickness of 1.0 ± 0.1 mm to obtain optical quality surfaces for further coating and characterization studies. Consequently, synthesized tellurite and silicate glasses are coded according to their Dy3+ concentration as TDx and SDx (x presents mol % of Dy3+), respectively. Digital images of optically prepared samples are shown in Fig. S3 and nominal compositions of synthesized samples are given in Table S1 along with the sample codes.

Herein, we present a series of experiments describing how to determine ideal Dy3+ doping concentrations before BCDs coating studies. As depicted in Fig. S4a and Fig. S4b, PL emission intensities of tellurite and silicate glasses show an increment until Dy3+ doping concentrations reach 3 mol % and 0.5 %, respectively. However, a further increase in Dy3+ doping concentrations results in a decrement in emission intensity values for TD4 and SD1 glass samples due to the PL quenching behavior. Therefore, TD3 and SD05 — exhibiting the highest PL intensity values — are selected as the samples with the optimum doping concentrations from tellurite and silicate glass systems, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of BCDs and BCDs/PVP solution
ce:section.ce:para: As previously reported elsewhere [36], the BCDs are prepared via a facile thermal method using carob molasses as a natural carbon source, polyethylene glycol (PEG) as a passivation agent, and urea as a nitrogen source (see Fig. 1b). The carob molasses is obtained from a local supermarket, while PEG and urea are purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Firstly, 1.0 g of carob molasses is diluted in 1:10 (w/v) ratio in deionized water. Then, 1 mL of this molasses solution is mixed with the PEG and urea (1:8:4 w/w) that are dispersed in 2 mL of (1:1 v/v) water/ethanol. The resulting mixture is heated at 250 °C for 45 min in a Teflon oven vessel. The obtained brown material is dissolved in 6 mL of deionized water and this suspension is centrifuged at 13500 rpm for 20 min to remove large particles. The supernatant is taken and vacuum-dried at 90 °C overnight. In the preparation of the BCDs/PVP solution, 1.5 g of poly vinylpyrrolidone (PVP) is dissolved in DMF in a magnetic stirrer for 15 min. Afterwards, 15 mg of BCDs are added to the obtained homogeneous PVP solution, sonicated for 5 min, and homogeneously dissolved in a magnetic stirrer for 15 min resulting in water-dispersible CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spin-coating of BCDs/PVP solution on Dy3+-doped luminescent glasses
ce:section.ce:para: Prepared glass samples are cleaned in an ultrasonic bath to remove any excess residue using acetone, distilled water, and ethanol consecutively. Afterwards, BCD-PVP solution (20 μL) is spin-coated at 3000 rpm for 30 s (spin coater: SCS G3P-8 Spin Coater) on selected glass samples, i.e., TD3 and SD05. BCD-coated glasses are then dried in an oven at 120 °C for 1 h to remove excess solvent, and this process is repeated until three layers of coatings — that is the number of coating layers to provide sufficient blue emission for an optimal white light generation — are deposited on glasses (see Fig. 1c). For clarity, BCD-coated glasses are labeled according to the applied number of coating layers, i.e., TD3–3 L and SD05–3 L. At this point, it is important to indicate that there are several forces acting on the coating layer and the glass substrate during the spin coating process such as adhesive, gravity, Van der Waals, centrifugal, shear and depletion forces. Among these, adhesive and centrifugal are the key forces that mainly control the spin-coating process. The adhesive force is resulted due to the interaction between BCDs solution and luminescent glass substrate. Strong sheering of BCDs solution occurs as a result of the adhesive force at the interface of BCDs solution and luminescent glass surface and then most of the BCDs solution flows from the luminescent glass substrate during spinning as a result of the centrifugal force. Ultimately, a thin liquid coating layer becomes uniform in thickness as a consequence of removal of excess solution [37–39]. There may be adhesion forces between the coating layer and the glass substrate. The most frequently acknowledged mechanism for describing adhesion between PVP and hydrogen-rich surfaces in close contact is molecular bonding, which includes intermolecular interactions like van der Waals force and hydrogen bonding. The van der Waals forces may be present to generate physical adsorption between the coating layer and glass substrate, whereas hydrogen bonding may also occur between electron donor N-CO groups of PVP on the BCDs/PVP and surface hydroxyls of the glass surface [40–43].

X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) (Specs-Flex XPS system) is used to characterize the surface functionalization and chemical composition of BCDs. The morphology of BCDs is characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (model JEOL USA JEM-1400). The average particle size and standard deviation are calculated by measuring the diameter of at least 100 particles using the ImageJ program. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) is conducted to confirm the thermal stability of synthesized BCDs/PVP solution using a Mettler Toledo Thermal Analysis System TGA/DSC 3 + with a heating rate of 10 K/min from 25° to 800°C under a nitrogen atmosphere at a flow rate of 40 mL/min (see Fig. S5).

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) imaging is used for morphology analysis by coating the sample surface with Au/Pd on a Zeiss EVO LS 10 instrument operating at 5 kV. Additionally, cross-sectional SEM micrographs are used to determine the thickness of coatings. The optical absorption spectra of glass samples together with BCDs/PVP solution are recorded in the wavelength range of 300–1000 nm using a PG T80 + UV-Vis spectrophotometer with a resolution of 0.1 nm. PL excitation and emission spectra of BCDs/PVP solution, uncoated and coated luminescent glasses are measured in an Edinburgh Instruments FS5 spectrofluorometer using a 150 W xenon lamp as an excitation source with high spectral resolution (signal to noise ratio of water Raman signal>6000:1). Absolute PL quantum yield (PLQY) values — defined as the ratio of the number of emitted photons to the absorbed photons — are measured with the same spectrofluorometer by using an integrating sphere (150 mm internal spherical cavity, polytetrafluoroethylene coated) under 365 nm excitation wavelength with an estimated error rate of± 5 %. PLQY measurements are repeated ten times for each sample and the average.

values are adopted to ensure data reliability. PL emission spectra of samples are used to determine the colorimetric properties such as Commission internationale de l′éclairage (CIE) 1931 color coordinates, color rendering index (CRI), correlated color temperature (CCT) and color purity values using OSRAM Color Calculator software as already explained in our previous studies [14,44]. Temperature-dependent PL measurements (stability measurements) are recorded up to five heating-cooling cycles by placing a Pike Technologies heated solid transmission attachment in the spectrofluorometer and by increasing the temperature from 30 °C to 120 °C and then cooling back down to 30 °C.

The elemental compositions and chemical functional groups of BCDs are identified by high-resolution XPS analysis. The deconvoluted C1s XPS spectrum displays three peaks at 283.18 eV, 284.74 eV, and 287.6 eV that can be ascribed to the C-C, C-C/CC, and CO groups, respectively (see Fig. 2a) [45–47]. On the other hand, the deconvoluted N1s XPS spectrum (see Fig. 2b) confirms the presence of pyridinic N (397.4 eV, 398.2 eV, and 398.9 eV) [48]. Finally, the deconvoluted O1s XPS spectrum (see Fig. 2c) is attributed to the CO (529.5 eV and 530.9 eV) and C-OH/C-O-C (531.6 eV) bonds [49,50].

The morphology and size of BCDs are characterized by TEM analysis. Fig. 3a shows a general bright-field TEM image which demonstrates the homogeneous distribution of BCDs over a large area with good dispersibility. Fig. 3b depicts the particle size distribution histogram of BCDs. As can be seen from this figure, the sizes of BCDs vary between 4 nm and 24 nm with an average diameter of 12.49 ± 4.10 nm.

The surface and cross-sectional SEM images are collected to check the quality of BCDs coatings on luminescent glasses. Homogeneous morphology of BCDs coatings with good dispersion properties can be observed from the surface SEM images for selected tellurite and silicate glasses (see Fig. S6a and
Fig. 6b). In addition, the analysis of cross-sectional images show that the thickness of the coatings is in the range of 900–1000 nm for TD3–3 L and SD05–3 L glasses (see Fig. 4a and Fig. 4b).

The absorbance spectrum together with the digital image of the BCDs/PVP solution is illustrated in Fig. S7. The absorbance spectra of uncoated and BCD-coated samples are given together to compare the change in optical properties of luminescent glasses after BCDs coating (see Fig. 5a and Fig. 5b). As can be seen clearly, no significant change is observed in absorbance behavior after coating BCDs on the glass surface. Absorbance spectra of uncoated TD3 and BCD-coated TD3–3 L glasses are presented in Fig. 5a. The five absorption peaks centered at 452 nm, 474 nm, 757 nm, 806 nm, and 905 nm are ascribed to the transitions of 6H15/2→4I15/2, 6H15/2→4F9/2, 6H15/2→6F3/2, 6H15/2→6F5/2 and 6H15/2→6F7/2 of Dy3+, respectively [51]. Transitions below 415 nm could not be observed due to the absorption edge of the TD3 glass — that shifts from 432 nm to approximately 439 nm when BCDs coating is applied. Fig. 5b shows absorption spectra of uncoated SD05 and BCD-coated SD05–3 L glasses with five absorption peaks of Dy3+ located at 389 nm, 452 nm, 751 nm, 800 nm, and 891 nm that are related to 6H15/2→4F7/2, 6H15/2→4I15/2, 6H15/2→6F3/2, 6H15/2→6F5/2 and 6H15/2→6F7/2 transitions, respectively [52]. The bands located at 389 nm and 452 nm are weaker in intensity due to the strong absorption of the glass matrix near the absorption edge. The absorption edge of SD05 glass is observed around 374 nm and slightly shifts to 379 nm with BCDs coating. The inset images given in Fig. 5a and Fig. 5b show that both glass types have a transparent and homogeneous coating on the surface under daylight.

PL excitation (PLE) measurements are conducted to find the most appropriate excitation wavelength where the materials emit their intense characteristic lights and also to find the optimum excitation wavelength to obtain pure white light emission. Accordingly, the PLE spectrum of BCDs/PVP solution is recorded by monitoring its characteristic blue emission at 450 nm. As seen in Fig. 6a BCDs are suitable for excitation over a wide range in the UV region. In addition, PLE spectra of TD3 and SD05 glasses are recorded by monitoring the characteristic yellow emission of Dy3+ at 575 nm. The PLE spectrum of TD3 glass displays three excitation peaks located at 351 nm, 365 nm, and 388 nm originating from 6H15/2 → 6P7/2, 6H15/2 → 6P5/2, and 6H15/2 → 4I13/2 transitions, respectively (see Fig. 6b) [53]. On the other hand, the excitation peaks centered at 324, 349, 363, and 386 nm for SD05 glass are attributed to the 6H15/2 → 6P3/2, 6H15/2 → 6P7/2, 6H15/2 → 6P5/2, and 6H15/2 → 4F7/2 transitions, respectively (see Fig. 6c) [54]. The PLE measurements reveal that BCD-coated luminescent glasses are suitable for efficient excitation in the UV region that matches well with the emission wavelengths of UV chips-based LEDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL emission measurements and colorimetric properties
ce:section.ce:para: PL emission mapping results under various excitation wavelengths for BCD-coated TD3–3 L and SD05–3 L glasses show that white light emission is obtainable only under 365 nm excitation (see Fig. S8). Hence, hereafter PL emission results are given for excitation under 365 nm. The PL emission behavior of the BCDs/PVP solution is presented in Fig. 7a. As seen, BCDs demonstrate a broad emission between 400 and 550 nm with an emission peak located at ~450 nm. Upon 365 nm excitation BCDs/PVP solution results in intense blue emission (see inset of Fig. 7a) with the color coordinates of (x = 0.21, y = 0.23) in the chromaticity diagram (see Fig. 7b).

PL emission and colorimetric properties of uncoated and BCD-coated TD3 and SD05 glasses are depicted together in Fig. 8 for a better comparison of the emission intensities. As seen in Fig. 8a and Fig. 8d, PL emission spectra of uncoated tellurite and silicate glasses consist of three emission bands located at 482 nm, 575 nm, and 663 nm corresponding to the 4F9/2 → 6H15/2 (blue), 4F9/2 → 6H13/2 (yellow) and 4F9/2 → 6H11/2 (red) transitions, respectively [55]. There is no significant difference in the shape or position of the emission bands for both glass systems. In addition, the hypersensitive transition of 4F9/2 → 6H13/2 is relatively more intense than those of insensitive transitions due to its strong dependence on the local environment of the host. When PL spectra of uncoated and BCDs-coated glasses are compared, a significant enhancement in PL intensity between 400 nm and 550 nm due to the presence of BCD-coating can be observed (see Fig. 8a and Fig. 8d). However, there is no shift observed in the center wavelengths of emission peaks for both glass systems when the BCDs coating is applied to the surface of Dy3+-doped glasses. Additionally, PL emission results of BCDs coated tellurite and silicate glasses show a systematical increment in intensity by increasing the number of coating layers (see Fig. S9a and Fig. S9b). It is obvious that BCDs aggregation, which is a typical behavior of aggregated fluorescent carbon dots due to AIQ, does not occur as there is no indication of quenching. Furthermore, macro images of samples under 365 nm excitation and CIE color coordinates (x,y) that are calculated from the intensities of emission spectral profiles are depicted in Figs. 8b-8e and Figs. 8c-8f, respectively to better understand the colorimetric properties of uncoated and BCD-coated tellurite and silicate glasses. The macro images demonstrate that uncoated glasses emit yellow color, while BCD-coated glasses emit white light under 365 nm excitation (see Fig. 8b and Fig. 8e). As shown in Fig. 8c and Fig. 8f, corresponding CIE color coordinates of tellurite and silicate glasses are located at (x = 0.41, y = 0.45) and (x = 0.38, y = 0.41), respectively and upon application of BCDs coatings they move towards the white region of the diagram with the CIE color coordinates of (x = 0.31, y = 0.33) and (x = 0.31, y = 0.34), respectively. However, examining the color coordinate change of BCD-coated glasses, it is observed that tellurite glass shows a further shift towards the white region of the diagram from its original coordinate, indicating that even if the same number of BCDs coating layers is applied the PL emission intensities of tellurite and silicate glasses behave differently.

Apart from the CIE color coordinates, the quality of white light is also evaluated in terms of colorimetric parameters such as CCT, CRI, and color purity. Table 1 lists the colorimetric properties of uncoated and BCD-coated glasses including PLQY values along with some other previously reported studies on Dy3+-doped glasses. Considering uncoated Dy3+-doped tellurite and silicate glasses synthesized in this work, measured CCT values are 3657 and 4044 K (resulting in neutral white light), CRI values are 8 and 6, color purity values are 64.8 % and 49.2 % and PLQY values are 3.10 % and 20.81 %, respectively. Remarkably, CCT values of selected BCD-coated tellurite and silicate glasses are calculated as 5518 and 5136 K, respectively and found to be comparable to the CCT value of commercial white LEDs (>4000 K) [56]. The CRI values of BCD-coated tellurite and silicate glasses show approximately a double increase reaching to 17 and 19, respectively (see Table 1). This confirms that the low CRI values of uncoated glasses are due to insufficient blue emission of Dy3+. A high CRI value (>80) is usually preferable when WLEDs are used in applications such as indoor lighting [57], therefore the CRI values of BCD-coated luminescent glasses should be further increased before they find use in practical applications. It is well known that pure white light has a color purity value of 0 %. A dramatic decrease is observed in color purity values of BCD-coated tellurite (7.7 %) and silicate (10.2 %) glasses getting close to the purity values of pure white light.

Fig. S10 displays PLQY spectra of BCDs/PVP solution, TD3–3 L and SD05–3 L glasses under 365 nm excitation. PLQY value of BCDs/PVP solution is measured as 20.23 %. Extraordinarily, PLQY values of BCD-coated TD3–3 L and SD05–3 L glasses are calculated as 5.62 % and 31.49 %, respectively. The higher PLQY values obtained from BCDs-coated glasses can be explained by the boosted emission of Dy3+ ions by BCDs coating — PLQY values are improved by including the overall amount of emissions originating from both luminescent glasses and BCDs coating. This implies a PLQY increment of nearly 50 % after BCDs coating for both glasses. For further clarification, it is evident that the mechanism of radiative energy transfer simply needs the overlapping of emission and absorption spectra. Both Dy 3+-doped tellurite and silicate glasses show an absorption band at 452 nm ascribed to 6H15/2→4I15/2 transition of Dy3+. Moreover, the BCDs/PVP solution has a broad PL spectrum with an emission peak located at ~450 nm. As the emission peak of BCDs/PVP and absorption peak of Dy3+ ion coincide nearly at the same wavelength (see Fig. S11), the emission from BCDs coating can be absorbed and re-emitted by Dy3+ ions. As a result, BCDs coated glasses exhibit enhanced PL emissions ultimately causing an increase in the PLQY values. Consequently, compared to other works in the literature (see Table 1), BCD-coated Dy3+-doped glasses show improved colorimetric properties, i.e., very close coordinates to the pure white light (x = 0.33, y = 0.33), higher CCT, and higher CRI, lower color purity and comparable or better PLQY values.

Thermal stability tests of BCDs-coated Dy3+-doped glasses are performed by recording temperature-dependent PL spectra of TD3–3 L and SD05–3 L glasses (see Fig. 9a and Fig. 9d). The emission intensities of BCDs coated glasses show no noticeable decrease after heating up to 120 °C. Additionally, thermal cycling experiments also demonstrate that the emission characteristics are highly reversible revealing that there is no significant reduction in PL intensity even after five heating-cooling cycles of TD3–3 L and SD05–3 L glasses (see Fig. 9b and Fig. 9d). Moreover, the moisture stability of TD3–3 L and SD5–3 L glasses is evaluated by comparing the PLQY results measured on different days (0, 10, 30, and 60 days) as presented in Fig. 9c and Fig. 9e. As can be clearly seen, there is no substantial change between the measured PLQY values of TD3–3 L and SD05–3 L glasses on different days. More importantly, both glasses retain ~90 % of their initial PLQY value even after 60 days. A slight decrease in PLQY values after 60 days is probably due to unsuitable storage circumstances. The results indicate that the coatings are relatively stable, however it may be necessary to extra encapsulate these coatings to prevent any further damage before these materials find use in commercial applications.

In summary, this work reports a novel route for obtaining white light emission by combining BCDs and Dy3+-doped luminescent glasses. In this approach, an approximately 1 µm thick layer of BCDs/PVP solution is spin-coated on Dy3+-doped tellurite and silicate glass surfaces as the blue-emitting component to improve the colorimetric properties of luminescent glasses by enhancing insufficient blue emission of Dy3+ ions. Upon 365 nm excitation, the CIE color coordinates of BCD-coated tellurite and silicate glasses shift towards (x = 0.31, y = 0.33) and (x = 0.31, y = 0.34), respectively confirming the achievement of pure white light emission. More importantly, upon BCD-coating CCT values change from neutral to cool white light, CRI values show approximately a double increase, color purity values exhibit a dramatic decrease and PLQY values increase nearly by 50 % for both Dy3+-doped tellurite and silicate glasses. The obtained results are very encouraging, revealing that BCD-coated Dy3+-doped luminescent glasses have a great potential to be used as a cheap and environmentally friendly alternative in the manufacturing of resin-free WLED applications.

Beyza Özlem: Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Ulaş Korkmaz: Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Visualization. Erdinç Erol: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Melis Özge Alas: Investigation, Visualization. Zeynep Meray: Investigation. Rükan Genç Altürk: Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Miray Çelikbilek Ersundu: Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Ali Erçin Ersundu: Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

The authors of this study credit Beyzanur Begde and Naji Vahedigharehchopogh for their assistance with digital imaging and Emirhan Özen and Yusuf Temürhan for their assistance with sample preparation studies.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Down-conversion luminescence and shielding parameters of Dy3+: NFBT glass for white LED and radiation applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Mariselvam and J. Liu, "Down-conversion luminescence and shielding parameters of Dy3+: NFBT glass for white LED and radiation applications," Optical Materials, vol. 114, p. 110997, 2021/04/01/ 2021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Role of excitation wavelength and dopant concentration on white light tunability of dysprosium doped titania-fluorophosphate glasses

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Ravi et al., "Role of excitation wavelength and dopant concentration on white light tunability of dysprosium doped titania-fluorophosphate glasses," Optical Materials, vol. 111, p. 110593, 2021/01/01/ 2021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structural and optical investigations on Dy3+doped lithium tellurofluoroborate glasses for white light applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Uma, K. Maheshvaran, K. Marimuthu, and G. Muralidharan, "Structural and optical investigations on Dy3+doped lithium tellurofluoroborate glasses for white light applications," Journal of Luminescence, vol. 176, pp. 15–24, 2016/08/01/ 2016.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-efficiency fluorescence radiation of Dy3+ in alkaline earth borate glasses

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.H. Xiong, L.F. Shen, E.Y. B. Pun, and H. Lin, "High-efficiency fluorescence radiation of Dy3+ in alkaline earth borate glasses," Journal of Luminescence, vol. 153, pp. 227–232, 2014/09/01/ 2014.
